review_summary: The segment gives enough transcript detail to assess core text-CNN ideas: local n-gram filters, padding, multiple filters, pooling, and convolution design choices. No separate clip is needed.

question_bank.question: What distinguishes a text CNN from a left-to-right recurrent model when building sentence representations?
choices: It computes features over local n-gram windows rather than only updating a prefix state sequentially.
It ignores word order completely and only averages all word vectors.
It represents every sentence with exactly one learned word vector.
It requires a parse tree before any word vectors can be combined.
question_bank.explanation: A text convolution slides local filters over neighboring tokens, so it can detect local patterns such as bigrams or trigrams without relying only on a running prefix representation.

question_bank.question: In a 1D convolution over word embeddings, what does a single filter compute at each position?
choices: A dot product between the filter weights and the current n-gram window, usually followed by bias and a nonlinearity.
A global average over all tokens in the sentence before applying a classifier.
A randomly selected word vector that replaces the current token.
A syntactic parse decision based only on part-of-speech tags.
question_bank.explanation: The filter is a learned set of weights applied to one local window at a time. Bias and a nonlinearity turn that local match score into an activation feature.

question_bank.question: Why is max pooling often a natural summary operation after convolutional filters have scanned a text?
choices: It keeps whether a learned feature fired strongly anywhere, which is useful for feature detection across positions.
It forces every filter to care equally about every token in the sentence.
It reconstructs the original word order after padding has been applied.
It removes the need for learned convolutional filters.
question_bank.explanation: If a filter detects a pattern such as a topic, style, or phrase type, the strongest activation tells the classifier that the pattern occurred somewhere, even if its exact position is not crucial.

question_bank.question: What is the effect of adding zero padding around a token sequence before applying a width-3 convolution?
choices: It can preserve the original sequence length by allowing windows to be centered near the sentence boundaries.
It guarantees that every output activation is exactly zero.
It removes all boundary words from the convolution output.
It changes the convolution from a local operation into a full-sentence operation.
question_bank.explanation: Without padding, a width-3 filter cannot form full windows at the edges, so the output shrinks. Padding supplies boundary placeholders so edge-centered windows can still be computed.
